QF.id: IV Fixed Income Instruments and Bond Markets_16

question.text: Two bonds have the same 10-year maturity: Bond X has a 6% coupon and Bond Y has a 9% coupon. Which bond has higher Macaulay Duration?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Macaulay Duration is inversely related to coupon rate, all else being equal. Higher coupon → more cash flow received earlier → lower weighted average time → lower duration. So Bond X (lower coupon 6%) has higher duration than Bond Y (9% coupon).
**The Trap:** Students sometimes confuse maturity with duration. While both bonds have the same maturity, their durations differ because of different coupon rates.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Duration-coupon relationships are a core topic in Series IV and tested across all derivative exams where hedge ratios are involved.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A zero-coupon bond has the highest possible duration for a given maturity — equal to its maturity. Every coupon payment brings duration below maturity.
